description: The presented image is an irrelevant software interface button with the text "Check for updates" below a logo composed of a blue-yellow partial circular ring and a red bookmark icon, set on a light gray square background. This image has no correlation with the content of the target paper focusing on Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 high entropy steel fabricated via mechanical alloying and spark plasma sintering, so no valid scientific information related to phase evolution, microstructure, mechanical properties or other research content of the high entropy steel can be extracted from this mismatched image.

This group of X-ray diffraction characterization results focuses on phase evolution of Fe₄₀Mn₁₉Ni₁₅Al₁₅Si₁₀C₁ high entropy steel powders during mechanical alloying. Subfigure (a) shows full-range (2θ = 20°–100°) XRD patterns of samples milled for 0 h to 35 h, tracking the decay of elemental Fe, Mn, Ni, Al, Si, C diffraction peaks as milling progresses. Subfigure (b) gives an enlarged view of the 2θ = 49°–54° region to resolve subtle peak changes during phase formation. Subfigure (c) displays the detailed XRD pattern of the 35 h milled sample, identifying coexisting phases: dominant BCC phase, γ-brass type phase, B2-type ordered intermetallic phase, and trace residual Si. Subfigure (d) provides Rietveld refinement results of the 35 h sample XRD data to validate phase composition and structural parameters. Key trends include sequential dissolution of alloying elements (Al fully dissolves after 15 h, Ni/Mn after 20 h), solid solution formation after 25 h, and formation of the expected nanocrystalline multiphase structure after 35 h of milling, supporting the effectiveness of the adopted mechanical alloying process.

This figure presents characterization results of Fe₄₀Mn₁₉Ni₁₅Al₁₅Si₁₀C₁ high entropy steel powders processed by mechanical milling for different durations. Subfigures (a-d) are SEM micrographs showing powder morphologies after 10 h, 20 h, 30 h, 35 h of milling respectively, where (d) is the full area selected for EDS analysis. Subfigure (e) is the EDS spectrum of 35 h milled powder, confirming the presence of Fe, Mn, Ni, Al, Si, C matching the nominal composition. Subfigures (f-i) are particle size distribution histograms, with average particle sizes of 10±2 μm (10 h), 6±2 μm (20 h), 5±1.5 μm (30 h), 4±1 μm (35 h), showing a continuous decrease of average particle size with extended milling time. Subfigure (j) and corresponding elemental mapping show homogeneous element distribution, with light grey contrast for Fe-rich BCC phase and dark grey for Mn-rich γ-brass structure. It confirms prolonged milling refines particles, achieves uniform elemental mixing and desired phase formation.

This figure presents the TEM characterization of Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 high entropy steel powder after 35 h of mechanical alloying. Subfigure (a) is the bright-field TEM image with a scale bar of 20 nm, showing that uniform nanocrystalline grains are formed in the milled sample. Subfigure (b) is the corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern with a scale bar of 10 1/nm, exhibiting typical polycrystalline ring features. These diffraction rings are indexed to the (110), (200), and (211) planes of the body-centered cubic (BCC) phase, confirming the polycrystalline nature and dominant BCC crystal structure of the 35 h milled sample. The results verify that mechanical alloying for 35 h successfully produces nanocrystalline BCC-structured high entropy steel powder, supporting the phase evolution conclusion of the mechanical alloying process for this alloy system.

This figure presents the Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA) thermogram of 35 h milled Fe₄₀Mn₁₉Ni₁₅Al₁₅Si₁₀C₁ high entropy steel powder, tested in the temperature range of 200 °C to 1000 °C. The y-axis denotes heat flow in μV/mg, with the downward direction indicating exothermic events. Two distinct exothermic peaks are clearly observed on the curve, located at 420 °C and 520 °C respectively. The exothermic event at ~420 °C corresponds to the ordering and/or texturing of the γ-brass type phase, while the peak at ~520 °C is attributed to the formation of Fe₅Si₃-type intermetallic phase. The thermogram demonstrates that the 35 h milled powder is thermally stable up to 500 °C, and phase transformations occur above this temperature, providing direct thermal behavior evidence for the phase evolution analysis of the alloy during subsequent annealing and sintering processes.

This group of ex-situ X-ray diffraction patterns characterizes phase evolution of 35 h milled Fe₄₀Mn₁₉Ni₁₅Al₁₅Si₁₀C₁ high entropy steel powder annealed at room temperature, 400 °C, 500 °C, 600 °C and 800 °C. Subplot (a) shows full-range XRD patterns in 2θ range 20°–100°, (b) shows magnified peaks in 42°–46° 2θ, (c–e) present deconvoluted peaks of samples annealed at 400 °C, 500 °C, 600 °C respectively in 43°–47° 2θ, and (f) shows magnified peaks of 800 °C annealed sample in 40°–46° 2θ. Identified phases include BCC, B2-type, γ-brass type, and Fe₅Si₃-type intermetallic. The milled powder is thermally stable up to 500 °C, Fe₅Si₃-type phase forms at 600 °C corresponding to the 520 °C exothermic event in DTA, and B2-type phase content increases at 800 °C. The figure supports the phase transformation rule of the high entropy steel powder during thermal annealing.

This set of figures presents X-ray diffraction characterization results of spark plasma sintered (SPSed) Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 high entropy steel. Subfigure (a) shows the full-range XRD pattern in the 2θ range of 30° to 100°, with identified diffraction peaks of four phases: major BCC (ferritic) matrix phase, B2-type ordered intermetallic, γ-brass type phase, and Fe5Si3-type intermetallic silicide. Subfigure (b) displays the deconvolution of overlapping diffraction peaks in the 51°-54° 2θ range, separating peaks of the four coexisting phases to further confirm their presence. Subfigure (c) is the Rietveld refinement plot, including observed data, calculated pattern, background, zero baseline, difference curve, and vertical bars marking peak positions of each phase. The well-matched refinement result verifies the coexistence of the four phases, and is used to determine lattice parameters, crystallite size and phase fractions of the SPSed sample. This figure confirms that the SPS-processed high entropy steel consists of BCC matrix, B2 ordered phase, minor γ-brass phase and Fe5Si3-type silicide precipitates.

This figure presents characterization results of spark plasma sintered (SPSed, 900 °C, 50 MPa) non-equiatomic Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 high entropy steel. Subfigures (a) and (b) are scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images at different magnifications, with scale bars of 5 μm and 3 μm respectively. Multiple contrast regions are observed: light grey (marked as 1), white (marked as 2), dark grey (marked as 3), and onion-shaped black/dark grey features, which correspond to BCC matrix, B2 intermetallic, γ-brass phase, and Fe5Si3-type silicide respectively. Subfigure (c) is the full-area energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) spectrum of the marked region in (b), confirming the presence of all constituent elements (C, Fe, Mn, Ni, Al, Si) as expected from the nominal composition. This figure verifies that the SPSed high entropy steel forms a multiphase microstructure with designed elemental components.

This group of figures includes 1 backscattered electron (BSE) microscopic image with a 50 μm scale bar (marked with two selected positions 1 and 2) and 5 electron probe microanalysis (EPMA) elemental mapping plots for Fe, Mn, Si, Ni, Al respectively, tested on the spark plasma sintered (SPSed) Fe₄₀Mn₁₉Ni₁₅Al₁₅Si₁₀C₁ high entropy steel sample. Fe, Mn and Ni exhibit almost uniform distribution in the matrix, corresponding to the dominant BCC matrix phase. Regions with high Al concentration also show higher Fe and Ni signal intensity, which is attributed to the existence of B2-type ordered intermetallic phase, while Si-enriched regions correspond to Fe-Si type silicide phase. The γ-brass type phase is difficult to distinguish from these maps for its extremely low volume fraction, which matches well with XRD results. This figure confirms the multiphase microstructure of the SPSed high entropy steel, supporting the phase composition conclusion of the studied material system.

This figure presents the mechanical properties of spark plasma sintered (SPSed) Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 low-density high entropy steel. (a) is the indentation load vs depth p-h curve, deriving microhardness of 7.8 ± 0.3 GPa and elastic modulus of 184 ± 5 GPa. (b) is the optical micrograph of the Vickers indent under 5000 mN load, with 20 μm scale bar, showing no crack formation indicating good damage resistance. (c) is the compressive engineering stress-strain curve, giving ultimate compressive strength of 2046 ± 160 MPa with ~19% compressive strain. (d) is the radar chart summarizing multiple mechanical properties of the sample. (e) and (f) compare the microhardness, compressive strength of the present work with previously reported high entropy alloys and conventional steels as a function of density: the present alloy exhibits much higher hardness and compressive strength at a low density of ~6.5 g/cc than most reference materials, demonstrating its excellent mechanical performance advantage for low-density structural applications.

This figure is a CALPHAD-modelled phase amount versus temperature diagram for Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 high entropy steel. The x-axis represents temperature ranging from ~500 K to 1500 K, while the y-axis denotes the molar amount of all phases. Six curves correspond to distinct phases: 1) B2_VACANCY(AL:NI) (B2 ordered phase, ~0.28 mol at low temperature, gradually decreasing as temperature rises); 2) BCC_A2(FE,MN:VA) (dominant phase, maintaining 0.6-0.75 mol between 600 K and ~1100 K, then decreasing to nearly 0 at 1500 K); 3) BCC_A2#2(MN:VA,C) (trace phase with content <0.05 mol, stable up to ~1100 K); 4) LIQUID(FE,MN) (initiates formation at ~1100 K, rising to 1 mol at 1500 K); 5) M3SI(MN,FE:SI) (silicide phase, ~0.1 mol at low temperature, disappearing above ~750 K); 6) BCC_A2#3(FE:VA) (~0.45 mol below 600 K, fully vanishing at ~600 K). The diagram predicts the alloy is dominated by BCC_A2 and B2 phases below 1100 K with minor secondary phases, and fully melts at 1500 K, providing thermodynamic reference for experimental phase evolution analysis of the high entropy steel.
